mappings.rationaleAI: Development of the modern state from ancient citizenship through democratic governance as types of political systems (A.2), political parties and institutional balance as branches of government (B), and examination of the nation-state system (A).

Representative and direct democracy as types and models of political systems (A.2), parliamentary rule as concentration of legislative and executive functions (B.1), and the legislature in democratic governance (B.2).

Military, single-party, and personalist regimes as types and models of political systems contrasting with democratic forms (A.2), covering how authoritarian structures organize governmental authority.

Federal state systems as a structure of national government with power-sharing between national and subnational levels (A.1.b), and state and local governments as subnational political systems (A.1.d).
The EU as a supranational political system comprising a regional federation (A.1.a), and its institutional structure including legislative processes as an example of supranational legislature (B.2).

The EU legal framework as a supranational political system with treaty-based governance (A.1.a), and EU courts interpreting and applying law as an example of judiciary function at the supranational level (B.4).
The UN functions as a supranational world congress (A.1.a), mediating sovereignty and collective action among member states.

Empire as a supranational political system with hierarchical governance over diverse peoples (A.1.a), and imperial formations as a type and model of political system from Rome through modern neo-imperialism (A.2).
The Holy Roman Empire as a supranational political system spanning from Charlemagne to 1806 (A.1.a), and its constitutional structures and legal traditions as a type of political system (A.2).
The British Empire as a supranational political system encompassing global territorial administration and the role of the East India Company in imperial governance (A.1.a).
Dynastic rule as a type and model of political system based on bloodline-based legitimacy, examining how ruling families organized governmental authority across civilizations (A.2).

The presidency within the federal state system of the United States (A.1.b), and the Executive Office including the National Security Council and policy execution as the executive branch of government (B.3).
Congress as the legislative branch within the federal state system (A.1.b), covering the bicameral structure, committee systems, floor proceedings, and legislative functions (B.2).
The Supreme Court as the judiciary branch of the federal government (B.4), covering judicial review power, case selection, and the Court's institutional evolution over two centuries.
The American court system at state and federal levels as the judiciary (B.4), including trial courts, appellate courts, and jurisdiction within the federal state system (A.1.b).

The constitutional framework establishing federalism and separation of powers as the federal state system (A.1.b), and the structure of government branches including legislative, executive, and judicial functions (B).

Core American political institutions including the Presidency (B.3), Congress (B.2), the judiciary (B.4), and independent agencies within the federal state system (A.1.b), as well as state and local governments as subnational political systems (A.1.d).

British parliamentary democracy as a national political system (A.1.b), its unwritten constitution as a type and model of political system (A.2), and parliamentary governance as concentration of legislative and executive functions (B.1).

The British constitutional system as a unitary nation-state (A.1.b), its unusual unwritten arrangement as a distinctive model of political system (A.2), parliamentary sovereignty as concentration of legislative and executive functions (B.1), and the House of Commons and Lords as the legislature (B.2).

Governance across corporate, public, and global spheres as political systems at multiple levels (A), and international organizations including the World Bank, IMF, and UN as elements of supranational political systems (A.1.a).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Stella Z. Theodoulou, Ravi K. Roy
mappings.rationaleAI: Government bureaucracy developing and delivering public services as part of the executive branch (B.3), and comparative models of administration across different political systems (A).

Urban planning addressing the administration and governance of cities as urban governments (A.1.c), including metropolitan growth, regional consolidation, and the intersection of public policy with urban community design.

The Roman Republic as a type and model of political system (A.2), its political structures including the Senate and magistracies as legislature (B.2), and the cursus honorum as executive offices (B.3).
The Soviet single-party system as a type and model of political system (A.2), and Communist Party structure and central planning as the executive apparatus of the Soviet state (B.3).

Magna Carta as a foundational document shaping the development of constitutional political systems by limiting royal authority and establishing subjects' rights, contributing to the evolution of political system types (A.2).
